title: Spanish colonial authorities execute a man in Florida for sodomy
description: In 1566 Spanish colonial authorities in Florida put a Frenchman to death for homosexual acts, recorded as the earliest death sentence for same-sex activity in what is now the United States.

title: Carl von Westphal publishes early clinical writing on same-sex desire
description: In 1869 the German psychiatrist Carl von Westphal published one of the first medical accounts framing same-sex attraction as a clinical subject, an early step in the medicalization of homosexuality in European science.

title: Richard von Krafft-Ebing's sexology reaches medical readers
description: Around 1882 the psychiatrist Richard von Krafft-Ebing's writings on sexual behavior circulated among European medical audiences, shaping how physicians classified homosexuality. The date is approximate and may reflect his later Psychopathia Sexualis.

title: Havelock Ellis publishes sympathetic studies of homosexuality
description: In 1897 the British writer Havelock Ellis published influential studies that treated homosexuality with relative sympathy while describing same-sex attracted people as a distinct 'third sex', a framing that carried both progressive and pathologizing assumptions.

title: Harlem speakeasies bring gay nightlife to wider audiences
description: During the 1920s, blues performances and drag entertainment in Harlem and New York speakeasies exposed broad Prohibition-era audiences to gay culture. The decade date is approximate.

title: Nazi regime destroys Magnus Hirschfeld's Institute library
description: On 10 May 1933 the Nazi regime looted and destroyed the library and archives of Magnus Hirschfeld's Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin, erasing a major center of early sexology and homosexual rights research.

title: Alfred Kinsey publishes his report on human sexual behavior
description: Alfred Kinsey published his landmark report on male sexual behavior, proposing a spectrum model of sexual orientation from exclusively heterosexual to exclusively homosexual. The source dates it to 1947; the study is generally published as 1948.

title: Harry Hay founds the Mattachine Society
description: In 1950 Harry Hay, Chuck Rowland and others founded the Mattachine Society in Los Angeles, one of the earliest sustained homophile organizations for gay men in the United States.

title: Donald Webster Cory publishes The Homosexual in America
description: In 1951 Donald Webster Cory published The Homosexual in America, arguing that gay people form a legitimate minority group, an early and widely read case for civil rights framing.

title: One, Inc. is established as a homophile organization
description: In 1952 activists established One, Inc. on the West Coast, an early homophile organization that published One magazine and litigated for the right to distribute gay material through the mail.

title: Evelyn Hooker wins an NIMH grant to study gay men
description: In 1953 the psychologist Evelyn Hooker received a National Institute of Mental Health grant to study the psychological adjustment of gay men, funding research that would challenge the illness model of homosexuality.

title: Phyllis Lyon and Del Martin found the Daughters of Bilitis
description: In 1955 Phyllis Lyon and Del Martin founded the Daughters of Bilitis in San Francisco, the first sustained lesbian organization and support network in the United States.

title: Evelyn Hooker shows gay men are as well-adjusted as others
description: In 1956 Evelyn Hooker presented research finding no difference in psychological adjustment between gay and heterosexual men, providing key scientific evidence against classifying homosexuality as a mental disorder.

title: Kameny and Gittings lead early gay rights demonstrations
description: In 1965 Frank Kameny and Barbara Gittings led public gay rights demonstrations in Philadelphia and Washington, D.C., among the first organized protests of their kind in the United States.

title: The Stonewall uprising begins in Greenwich Village
description: On 28 June 1969 patrons of the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village fought back against a police raid, sparking days of protest that became a turning point for the gay liberation movement.

title: United Church of Christ ordains first openly gay minister
description: In 1972 the United Church of Christ ordained the first openly gay minister in a mainline American Protestant denomination, an early milestone for LGBTQ inclusion in organized religion.

title: PFLAG is founded to support families of LGBTQ people
description: In 1972 Parents and Friends of Lesbians and Gays (PFLAG) was founded, creating a national network for the families and allies of LGBTQ people in the United States.

title: American Psychiatric Association removes homosexuality from the DSM
description: In 1973 the American Psychiatric Association voted to remove homosexuality as a mental illness from its diagnostic manual, a decision that reshaped psychiatric practice and undercut the medical basis for discrimination worldwide.

title: First national march on Washington for gay rights
description: In 1979 the first National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights drew tens of thousands of demonstrators to the U.S. capital, marking the movement's arrival on the national political stage.

title: Second national march on Washington draws a million
description: In 1987 roughly one million people joined the National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights, held amid the AIDS crisis and demands for a federal response.

title: 1993 national march on Washington draws a million
description: In 1993 approximately one million people attended the March on Washington for Lesbian, Gay and Bi Equal Rights and Liberation, one of the largest civil rights demonstrations of the era.

title: Ellen DeGeneres comes out on national television
description: In April 1997 the comedian Ellen DeGeneres came out as a lesbian on national television, a widely watched moment that advanced LGBTQ visibility in American popular culture.

title: Vermont becomes the first state to recognize civil unions
description: In 2000 Vermont became the first U.S. state to grant same-sex couples the legal status of civil unions, extending many of the rights of marriage.

title: Lawrence v. Texas strikes down U.S. sodomy laws
description: In 2003 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Lawrence v. Texas that laws criminalizing consensual same-sex conduct were unconstitutional, decriminalizing homosexuality nationwide.

title: Massachusetts performs the first legal same-sex marriages
description: In 2004 Massachusetts became the first U.S. state to license same-sex marriages, following a state high court ruling that barring them was unconstitutional.

title: Obergefell v. Hodges legalizes same-sex marriage nationwide
description: In June 2015 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Obergefell v. Hodges that same-sex couples have a constitutional right to marry, extending marriage equality to every U.S. state.

title: Caitlyn Jenner publicly comes out as transgender
description: In 2015 Caitlyn Jenner publicly came out as a transgender woman, an event that drew wide media attention and raised transgender visibility in the United States.

title: Gunman attacks the Pulse nightclub in Orlando
description: On 12 June 2016 a gunman attacked the Pulse nightclub in Orlando during a Latin night, killing 49 people, most of them young LGBTQ Latinos, in one of the deadliest anti-LGBTQ attacks in U.S. history.
